questions.question: A radiograph archived for several years slowly yellows and its image fades. The residual chemical responsible and the compound it forms are:
options: Residual developer, forming metallic silver
Residual thiosulfate (hypo), forming silver sulfide
Residual wetting agent, forming silver halide
Residual stop-bath acid, forming silver nitrate
questions.explanation: Thiosulfate left behind by poor fixing or washing slowly converts the silver image to yellow-brown silver sulfide - the classic staining-and-fading failure of an archived film.

questions.question: An archive room runs at 85 °F and 70 % relative humidity through the summer. The stored radiographs face:
options: No particular risk - warmth keeps the emulsion flexible
Mold growth, films sticking together, and accelerated sulfide staining
Increased radiographic contrast over time
Static discharge marks appearing in storage
questions.explanation: Long-term storage wants roughly 60-75 °F and 30-50 % RH; heat accelerates the residual-hypo reaction and humidity above about 60 % grows mold and glues emulsions together.

questions.question: Which filing practice both protects archived film and guarantees retrieval?
options: Flat stacks sorted by film size with no index
On-edge filing in archival envelopes labeled with job, weld, date, and view, tracked in a master index and separated by contract and retention date
Taping each film into the back of its paper report
Keeping all films loose in a single fire-safe drawer
questions.explanation: On-edge filing in edge-glued archival envelopes prevents pressure and stain damage, while labeling, a master index, and separation by contract and retention date make any film producible in minutes.

questions.question: An exposure chart for an Ir-192 source was computed 74 days ago and never corrected. Using its exposure times today, the films will come out:
options: About twice as dense as intended
Correct - decay charts do not age
At roughly half the intended exposure - thin, with insufficient density
Foggy, with high base density
questions.explanation: 74 days is one half-life for Ir-192, so the source now delivers half the output the chart assumed - every film receives about half the intended exposure and runs thin.

questions.question: On a tapered part, the thin end images nearly black and the thick end nearly clear, with only a narrow readable band between. The standard cures are:
options: Lower the kilovoltage and extend development
Raise the kilovoltage for more latitude, or load two films of different speeds in the same cassette
Move the source closer to the part
Switch to a finer-grain film of the same speed
questions.explanation: The symptom is excessive contrast: the technique cannot span the thickness range. More energy widens latitude, and the double-film technique lets one film read the thick section while the other reads the thin.

questions.question: Dark, branching, lightning-like lines appear on films that were unloaded quickly on a cold, dry night. They are:
options: Crack images in the weld
Static discharge marks from sliding the film fast in dry air
Developer streaks from poor agitation
Crimp marks from rough loading
questions.explanation: Static discharge exposes the emulsion in branching, tree-like traces - a handling artifact driven by low humidity and fast film movement, not anything in the part.

questions.question: A setup's geometric unsharpness is twice the code limit. Without changing the source size or the object-to-film distance, you can halve Ug by:
options: Halving the source-to-object distance
Doubling the source-to-object distance
Doubling the exposure time
Switching to a faster film
questions.explanation: Ug = F·t/d, so unsharpness is inversely proportional to the source-to-object distance - doubling d cuts Ug in half (at the cost of roughly four times the exposure).

questions.question: During start-of-shift verification, the densitometer reads 2.08 on a certified 2.00 step. With a ±0.05 tolerance, the correct action is:
options: Note the offset and read films anyway
Adjust or recalibrate the instrument and re-verify before measuring any production film
Subtract 0.08 from each production reading
Verify against a thicker step where the error is smaller
questions.explanation: An out-of-tolerance instrument is out of service: it must be adjusted or repaired and successfully re-verified against the traceable tablet before any film is judged with it.

questions.question: How much more viewing light passes through a film area at density 1.0 than through an area at density 3.0?
options: 3 times more
10 times more
100 times more
1000 times more
questions.explanation: Density is logarithmic: D = 1.0 transmits 10 % and D = 3.0 transmits 0.1 %, a ratio of 100 - each density unit is a factor of ten in light.

questions.question: Which verification pairing reflects typical code practice for density measurement tools?
options: Densitometer verified annually; comparison strip verified each shift
Densitometer verified each shift, after 8 hours of use, or after an aperture change within ±0.05; comparison strip verified annually against a calibrated densitometer
Both verified only when purchased
Densitometer never verified if digital; comparison strip verified monthly
questions.explanation: The quantitative instrument gets frequent verification against a traceable step tablet (±0.05), while the visual comparison strip is verified at least annually against a calibrated densitometer.
